--- SOURCE 2: Urban Heat Islands and Energy Inequality in Syracuse, New York ---
URL: https://nysclimateimpacts.org/explore-the-assessment/case-studies/urban-heat-islands-and-energy-inequality/

SUMMARY:
Urban heat islands cause disproportionate energy burdens for people living in neighborhoods that are low-income and that have higher populations of people of

FULL CONTENT:
![New York State Climate Impacts Assessment](https://nysclimateimpacts.org/wp-content/themes/nyscia-theme/img/nyscia-logo.svg)

# Case Studies

![Blue lightbulb icon](https://i0.wp.com/nysclimateimpacts.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/nyscia-sector-energy-icon-blue.png?resize=268%2C268&ssl=1)

# Urban Heat Islands and Energy Inequality in Syracuse, New York

*Urban heat islands cause disproportionate energy burdens for people living in neighborhoods that are low-income and that have higher populations of people of color. To help combat the urban heat island effect, Syracuse adopted an Urban Forestry Master Plan to provide cooling where it is needed most.*

Climate change is expected to bring a host of environmental threats and ecological changes across New York State, including extreme heat and heat waves.1,2 The effects of rising temperatures and extreme heat can be more pronounced in urban areas due to a phenomenon known as the urban heat island effect. Heat islands are urbanized areas that experience higher temperatures due to buildings, roads, and other infrastructure that absorb and re-emit the sun’s heat more than natural landscapes such as forests and water bodies. Areas with highly concentrated structures and limited greenery become “islands” of higher temperatures, relative to outlying areas. Elevated temperatures from heat islands can adversely affect a community’s environment and quality of life in multiple ways, especially through the need for increased energy consumption for cooling.3

In Syracuse, as in other cities across New York State, the effects of heat islands can have immense environmental and energy justice implications. Due to 20th-century planning decisions (e.g., redlining, highway siting), the effects of heat islands in Syracuse are felt unevenly across neighborhoods. Neighborhoods that are low-income and have higher populations of people of color have more heat-absorbing buildings and pavements and fewer cool spaces with trees and greenery. For example, Interstate 81 was sited disproportionately through majority-Black neighborhoods in Syracuse.4 The impervious, heat-trapping surface of the highway—along with the lack of temperature-regulating land cover such as trees, greenspace, and waterways—has made the neighborhoods near Interstate 81 more vulnerable to extreme temperatures.

## Highlights

![Cars driving along a highway that is adjacent to residential neighborhoods.](https://i0.wp.com/nysclimateimpacts.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Interstate-81-in-Syracuse-NY_UHI-pic-1.jpg?resize=1200%2C800&ssl=1)

Increasing temperatures and legacies of environmental and socioeconomic inequality, along with emerging environmental threats, could converge and amplify energy inequality in Syracuse and beyond. The U.S. Department of Energy’s [Energy Justice Dashboard](https://energyjustice.egs.anl.gov/) has identified 15 census tracts within Syracuse as “disadvantaged communities” due to pervasive socioeconomic inequality and vulnerability. These tracts generally rank in the 80th and 90th-plus percentile nationally for categories that inform energy vulnerability, including household income, energy burdens (the percent of income dedicated to energy services), linguistic isolation, percent renters, and age of housing. Households in these tracts that already suffer from economic hardships often must rely on air conditioning more than other neighborhoods, leading to higher energy costs and energy burdens.

While it is vital that local and state energy policy address energy burdens, strategies are already underway to relieve energy inequality and urban heat islands. In 2020, to help address these issues, Syracuse adopted the [Syracuse Urban Forest Master Plan](https://www.syr.gov/files/sharedassets/public/2-departments/parks-recreation/documents/forestry/urban-forest-master-plan.pdf). The goal of this plan is to plant tens of thousands of trees, many in environmental justice communities. Trees and vegetation can combat excessive urban temperature disparities by providing needed cooling while also providing a host of local socioeconomic and environmental benefits, including lower energy demand and costs, workforce development associated with plantings and maintenance, air purification, erosion reduction, and flood control. Trees alone will not prevent urban heat islands in Syracuse; however, in concert with broader energy, public health, and environmental policy, they will help the city transition to a safer, healthier, and more just future.

![Cover of the Syracuse Urban Forest Master Plan with an aerial view of the city and the City of Syracuse's logo.](https://i0.wp.com/nysclimateimpacts.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Urban_forest_master_plan.png?resize=1024%2C793&ssl=1)

## References

1. New York State Department of Environmental Conservation. (n.d.). *Climate change effects and impacts*. Retrieved July 3, 2023, from <https://dec.ny.gov/environmental-protection/climate-change/effects-impacts>

2. Columbia University. (2023). *New York State climate change projections methodology report*. <https://nysclimateimpacts.org/explore-the-assessment/new-york-states-changing-climate/#Data>

3. U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. (n.d.). *Heat island effect*. Retrieved September 7, 2022, from <https://www.epa.gov/heatislands>

4. Teron, L. (2022). Deconstructing inequality: Cumulative impacts, environmental justice, and interstate redevelopment. *Richmond Public Interest Law Review*, *25*(3), 127–148. <https://scholarship.richmond.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1538&context=pilr>

--- SOURCE 3: Urban Heat Island Effect Solutions and Funding ---
URL: https://www.nlc.org/article/2023/02/13/urban-heat-island-effect-solutions-and-funding/

SUMMARY:
Many cities are experiencing the urban heat island effect, which has tremendous impacts on residents as climate changes increase the frequency and intensity of extreme heat events. Research from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) has been mapping the urban heat island effect in more than 60 cities across the country and has found that poor communities tend to be hotter than wealthier neighborhoods. NLC has created the tool below to assist local governments in mitigating the urban heat island effect in their own community by visualizing which bus stops should be prioritized. In the default “city view” page, each point represents city averages for tree canopy and heat risk, calculated by averaging all the individual bus stops within the city.

FULL CONTENT:
[*Skip to Content*](#main)







# Urban Heat Island Effect Solutions and Funding

* [Share this page on Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nlc.org%2Farticle%2F2023%2F02%2F13%2Furban-heat-island-effect-solutions-and-funding%2F)
* [Share this page on Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nlc.org%2Farticle%2F2023%2F02%2F13%2Furban-heat-island-effect-solutions-and-funding%2F)
* [Share this page on LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.nlc.org%2Farticle%2F2023%2F02%2F13%2Furban-heat-island-effect-solutions-and-funding%2F&title=&mini=true)

## By:

* Joshua Pine
* Kelly Aves
* Kyle Funk
* Zuhayr Ahmed
* Kyra Kocis

February 13, 2023 - (6 min read)

* [Climate Change](https://www.nlc.org/topic/climate-change/)
* [Infrastructure](https://www.nlc.org/topic/infrastructure/)
* [Sustainability & Resilience](https://www.nlc.org/topic/sustainability-resilience/)
* [Technology](https://www.nlc.org/topic/technology/)

Many cities are experiencing the urban heat island effect, which has tremendous impacts on residents as climate changes increase the frequency and intensity of extreme heat events. According to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), [Urban Heat Islands](https://www.epa.gov/green-infrastructure/reduce-urban-heat-island-effect) occur when municipalities replace green space with an urban environment such as pavement, buildings, and other surfaces that absorb and retain heat. Concentrated urban heat islands in cities can increase energy costs, air pollution levels, heat-related illness and even resident mortality. [Climate change](https://www.ipcc.ch/report/ar6/wg2/) is only going to increase these effects as heat projections for the next several decades continue to rise. The warming from climate change compounded with urban heat islands is already having a severe impact across the country, as over the last [30 years (1992-2021),](https://www.weather.gov/hazstat/) extreme heat caused more fatalities compared to other extreme weather events such as floods, tornadoes, cold and hurricanes.

Research from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) has been mapping the [urban heat island effect in more than 60 cities](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2022-11-28/citizen-scientists-map-urban-heat-down-to-the-block-level) across the country and has found that poor communities tend to be hotter than wealthier neighborhoods. Often these low-income neighborhoods line up with historical redlining policies, thus disproportionately harming Black communities and communities of color. To alleviate these disparities, cities can look at [heat equity](https://www.epa.gov/heatislands/heat-islands-and-equity), which is the development of policies and practices that can mitigate the urban heat island effect. Along with low-income neighborhoods, other groups like older adults, outdoor workers, and people who live alone or are homeless are also vulnerable to urban heat. Special attention should be given to groups with existing medical conditions such as asthma, which is more common in low-income and minority groups, because heat on sunny days can react with air pollutants to form smog, a major trigger of asthma.

### Heat Resilience Solutions

To help with urban heat islands the [EPA recommends](https://www.epa.gov/heatislands/heat-island-cooling-strategies) communities invest in the following to mitigate the effects:

* Plant trees and increase vegetation
* Install green or cool roofs
* Replace normal pavement with cool pavement
* Think about adopting a [smart growth](https://www.epa.gov/smartgrowth)

The EPA also has [database](https://www.epa.gov/heatislands/heat-island-community-actions-database) of actions taken by communities to reduce heat islands. For example, the city of Saratoga Springs, NY’s urban forest project partners the city with a local non-profit to inventory city street and park trees. This inventory will then be used to develop an Urban Forest Master Plan. The city of Boston, MA has created a new forestry division within the Parks and Recreational Department as a part of the city’s [Urban Forest Plan](https://www.boston.gov/departments/parks-and-recreation/urban-forest-plan). The new division will work to maintain and increase the tree canopy throughout the municipality. Boston’s forestry initiative works in conjunction with their [Heat Resiliency Solutions](https://www.boston.gov/environment-and-energy/heat-resilience-solutions-boston) plan, a “framework to prepare Boston for hotter summers, with particular focus on Boston’s environmental justice neighborhoods.” Municipal leaders can also look to learning modules to assess and create solutions like the [Heat Action Platform](https://onebillionresilient.org/heat-action-platform/). Elected officials can also invest in transportation systems to reduce heat stress and impacts during heat events. [Phoenix, AZ](https://www.valleymetro.org/images/uploads/destinations_newsletter/dest06spring.pdf) has been working to improve their bus shelters to cool passengers as they wait for buses by designing them to provide maximum shade. Route prioritization between vulnerable areas and cooling centers is something else that local leaders can consider. More resources are available at [heat.gov](https://www.heat.gov/) and the [Climate Mapping for Resiliency and Adaptation](https://storymaps.arcgis.com/stories/5e482f11d2514191bb89c20638d98b3c).

Several funding sources exist to combat excess urban heat. The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) includes Neighborhood Access and Equity Grants, providing competitive grants for [transportation infrastructure](https://www.aceee.org/blog-post/2022/10/cities-can-boost-access-efficient-transportation-using-3-billion-new-climate-law) overhauls as well as explicit approval for urban heat mitigation projects; and [Environmental and Climate Justice Grants](https://www.epa.gov/environmentaljustice/environmental-justice-grants-funding-and-technical-assistance), which also includes mitigating urban heat as an eligible activity. The IRA also allocates $1.5 billion to the [U.S. Forest Service’s Urban and Community Forestry Program](https://www.fs.usda.gov/managing-land/urban-forests/ucf), which helps cities and towns plant trees through the State Urban Forest Resilience program and the National Urban Forestry Challenge Cost Share Program. Some federal agencies have ongoing grant programs in which funding can be used for many of the solutions used to combat urban heat. NOAA holds their annual [Urban Heat Island Mapping](https://www.heat.gov/pages/mapping-campaigns) campaign, which cities can apply for funding and technical assistance. Furthermore, your state is likely to have grants for tree planting – a simple google search will return relevant results for state and non-profit programs providing money to local governments. Lastly, NLC will be launching a new technical assistance program to support participating communities make resilient infrastructure the default in construction – stay tuned for more information and lessons learned.

### Tool Explanation and Guidance

NLC has created the tool below to assist local governments in mitigating the urban heat island effect in their own community by visualizing which bus stops should be prioritized. We focus on bus stops and their corresponding [heat wave risk](https://hazards.fema.gov/nri/heat-wave) to help address heat inequity, as vulnerable populations are more likely to take public transportation and live in areas with above national average heat wave risk. Along with heat wave risk, we also layer in tree canopy data, and you can see in the corresponding scatterplots the relationship between these two variables.

In the default “city view” page, each point represents city averages for tree canopy and heat risk, calculated by averaging all the individual bus stops within the city. You can toggle between the color scales for tree canopy cover or heat wave risk. On the city view scatterplot, the dotted line indicates cities’ national averages for both indicators, so you can see where your city’s bus stops are with respect to the average – useful data when applying for federal or state grants.

The “bus stop” view is best suited for exploring an individual city, which can be selected by the drop down menu in the upper left corner. In this view, each point on the map is an individual bus stop, which can also toggle between tree canopy cover and heat wave risk. On the bus stop view scatterplot, the dotted line indicates the national bus stop averages. By hovering over individual bus stops, you can view the corresponding census tract’s racial demographics and public transportation usage to further inform priority stops.

Lastly, you can also select for specific characteristics from the sliding scale bars in the top right corner, or by clicking and dragging over the scatterplot, which will filter the stops shown on the map.  For example, you can filter for stops on the map that suffer from above average heat risk and/or below average vegetation cover to determine priority areas of investment.

*Data Sources*

* [Tree Canopy – United States Forest Service](https://www.mrlc.gov/data/type/tree-canopy)
* [Heat Wave Risk – FEMA](https://hazards.fema.gov/nri/heat-wave)
* [Bus Stop – National Transit Database](https://www.transit.dot.gov/ntd)

*The above dashboard was created using data from February 2023. For more information on tool methodology, email [researchdatainquiries@nlc.org](mailto:researchdatainquiries@nlc.org).*

--- SOURCE 4: Urban heat waves imperil L.A.'s most vulnerable communities ---
URL: https://today.usc.edu/urban-heat-waves-los-angeles-vulnerable-communities-usc-research/

SUMMARY:
New USC research highlights the outsized risk for low-income communities as global warming raises temperatures and our cities become even more crowded.

FULL CONTENT:
![Beauty shot of fountain and Hahn Plaza at USC]()

## What are you looking for?

# Urban heat waves imperil L.A.’s most vulnerable communities

New USC research highlights the outsized risk for low-income communities as global warming raises temperatures and our cities become even more crowded.

People living in low-income, disadvantaged communities across much of urban Los Angeles lack the means to cool their homes, posing a growing threat to their health — and their lives — from extreme heat waves due to global warming, new USC research shows.

The communities across southern L.A. are most vulnerable, underscoring social inequities as a warming world affects populations differently. The USC research aims to help prepare the megalopolis for big disruptions, manifest in the coronavirus crisis today and climate change tomorrow.

“We studied how residential electricity use may increase in a warming world,” said [Kelly Sanders](https://pressroom.usc.edu/kelly-sanders/), an author of the study and associate professor of civil and environmental engineering at the USC Viterbi School of Engineering. “These will be the people and neighborhoods least likely to be resilient to climate change because it’s harder for people in low-income communities to adapt.”

COVID-19 adds urgency, Sanders said, because “it has underscored the importance of preparing for shocks, particularly those that disproportionately impact underserved populations. We know that extreme heat events are increasing over time, and we hope that our work can be used to direct resources towards building resilience to warming in the vulnerable communities that might suffer most.”

The study was published today in [Environmental Research Letters](https://iopscience.iop.org/journal/1748-9326).

## Rising temperatures, crowded cities create infrastructure challenges

Hot cities pose serious challenges for policymakers in a warming planet. Heat is already a big killer, accounting for more deaths each year in the United States than storms, floods and lightning combined, the study says.

[![urban south LA heat graphic](http://today.usc.edu/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/urban_heat_graphic-scaled-1.jpg)](http://today.usc.edu/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/urban_heat_graphic-scaled-1.jpg)

(Graphic/K Selnick)

![urban south LA heat graphic](http://today.usc.edu/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/urban_heat_graphic-scaled-1.jpg)

Last year was the second hottest year on record, according to the [World Meteorological Organization](https://public.wmo.int/en/media/press-release/wmo-confirms-2019-second-hottest-year-record). The United Nations reports thousands of heat-related deaths or hospitalizations annually, raising concern among public health officials as extreme temperature events grow in intensity, frequency and duration.

Cities are becoming more crowded, too, as more than half of the world’s population now lives in metro areas. The portion is forecast to grow to nearly 70% in 30 years. And cities face a double whammy due to an urban [heat island effect](https://www.epa.gov/heat-islands), which occurs because the built environment retains more heat than natural environments.

Those trends pose challenges for electricity infrastructure and peak electricity management, underscoring the need to identify vulnerable communities. To anticipate future cooling needs, the study quantifies where urban warming is expected to increase air conditioning and energy use.

The USC research team turned to big data to tackle the problem.

First, they utilized smart meter data for 180,476 households in Southern California. Then, they quantified the increases in residential electricity use per degree of warming for census tracts in the region; that resulted in analyzing 3 billion electrical consumption records. They also created a method to estimate the prevalence of air conditioning units, which showed that the units are less common in poorer census tracts. Next, they utilized global warming projections for the region for 2070-2099.

“Our research suggests that more than half (55%) of the census tracts identified as most vulnerable are expected to experience more than 16 extreme heat days above 95 F per year by the end of the century,” said [George Ban-Weiss](https://pressroom.usc.edu/george-ban-weiss/), an author of the study and professor of civil and environmental engineering at USC Viterbi.

Many communities clustered in southern L.A. — including Lynwood, Compton, Inglewood and South Gate — lack the advantages to cope with hot temperatures found in other parts of the region, making them more vulnerable to extremes, the study shows.

## Research data reinforce how global warming, heat affect vulnerable communities

The study offers rich detail and data, as the analysis accounts for census tracts across Southern California — data only made possible by advances in electricity meters.

“Before we had these household-level smart meter data, we couldn’t locate the most vulnerable communities and where help is needed most,” said [Mo Chen](https://s3research.usc.edu/about-us/mo-chen/), lead author of the study and a doctoral student at USC Viterbi.

The study underscores a growing body of research that shows the uneven impacts of global warming. For example, across Southern California, coastal communities are somewhat buffered by the ocean while wealthy communities can afford more energy use and many homes in inland valleys mostly have air conditioners installed.

“The findings are useful from a policy perspective because they give policymakers information to direct resources — such as public funding, weatherization programs and cooling centers — towards the vulnerable populations that will be most negatively affected by extreme events in the future,” Sanders said.

*The study authors include Chen, Sanders and Ban-Weiss. Support for the study comes from National Science Foundation Chemical, Bioengineering, Environmental and Transport Systems grant (1512429), EAGER grant (1632945) and CAREER grants (1752522, 1845931) as well as the Fu “Dave” Yen Fellowship in Environmental Engineering award and Theodore & Wen-Hui Chen Fellowship to Chen.*

--- SOURCE 5: Surface urban heat island effects intensify more rapidly in lower ... ---
URL: https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9

SUMMARY:
Surface urban heat island effects intensify more rapidly in lower income countries | npj Urban Sustainability As urbanization proceeds globally in recent decades, a nuanced understanding of the differential impacts of the urban heat island (UHI) effect across income country groups is crucial. Due to rapid global urbanization, especially in developing countries, the urban heat island (UHI) effect has become increasingly severe1."),2."),3."). The majority of urban grids exhibiting a SUHII increasing trend during the daytime are observed in the US, Japan, and China, mainly located in climate zone C (temperate) and the high-income country group. In contrast, during nighttime, the urban areas exhibiting an increasing trend are predominantly distributed in climate zone C and the upper-middle-income country group, with China and the US showing the greatest global increase in SUHII.

Surface urban heat island effects intensify more rapidly in lower income countries

Abstract
--------

As urbanization proceeds globally in recent decades, a nuanced understanding of the differential impacts of the urban heat island (UHI) effect across income country groups is crucial. Through an analysis of ~5 million urban grids from 2003 to 2018, we discovered that the average surface urban heat island intensity (SUHII) increased at a rate of 0.021 °C annually. Despite the extensive areas of SUHII increase in higher-income countries, particularly the US and China, it is the low and lower-middle-income countries that exhibited a more pronounced net increase in intensity. Specifically, 27% of urban grids in low-income countries witnessed the most substantial net increase during the daytime, while 19% of urban grids in lower-middle-income countries led the surge at nighttime. Higher-income countries’ SUHII growth reflects their pace of urbanization, but the trend of intensifying urban heat expands faster in lower-income economies. Such findings underscore an impending UHI challenge for lower-middle and low-income nations, emphasizing the need for targeted mitigation and adaptation strategies.

Introduction
------------

Due to rapid global urbanization, especially in developing countries, the urban heat island (UHI) effect has become increasingly severe[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR1 "Angel, S., Parent, J., Civco, D. L., Blei, A. & Potere, D. The dimensions of global urban expansion: estimates and projections for all countries, 2000-2050. Prog. Plan. 75, 53–107 (2011)."),[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR2 "Li, K. & Chen, Y. Identifying and characterizing frequency and maximum durations of surface urban heat and cool island across global cities. Sci. Total Environ. 859, 160218 (2023)."),[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR3 "Liu, Z. et al. Surface warming in global cities is substantially more rapid than in rural background areas. Commun. Earth Environ. 3, 219 (2022)."). UHI effect refers to the phenomenon whereby urban areas experience higher temperatures than surrounding non-urban areas due to heat accumulation from human activities and heat transfer from other sources[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR4 "Oke, T. R. The energetic basis of the urban heat island. Q. J. R. Meteorol. Soc. 108, 1–24 (1982)."),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR5 "Howard, L. The Climate of London, Deduced from Meteorological Observations, Made in the Metropolis, and at Various Places Around it (1833)."). The global warming trend of the past century has been unprecedented in the last 24 thousand years[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR6 "Osman, M. B. et al. Globally resolved surface temperatures since the Last Glacial Maximum. Nature 599, 239–244 (2021)."). As temperatures continue to rise, the frequency and severity of extreme high-temperature events are expected to increase, which could lead to a range of negative effects that endanger human life[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR7 "McMichael, A. J., Woodruff, R. E. & Hales, S. Climate change and human health: present and future risks. Lancet 367, 859–869 (2006)."),[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR8 "Witze, A. The deadly impact of urban heat. Nature 595, 349–351 (2021)."), compromise the ecosystem[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR9 "Deutsch, C. A. et al. Impacts of climate warming on terrestrial ectotherms across latitude. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 105, 6668–6672 (2008)."), cause significant damage to infrastructure, and result in enormous economic losses[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR10 "Deilami, K., Kamruzzaman, M. & Liu, Y. Urban heat island effect: A systematic review of spatio-temporal factors, data, methods, and mitigation measures. Int. J. Appl. Earth Obs. Geoinf. 67, 30–42 (2018)."). Given the projected increase in urban population, with 68% of the global population expected to reside in urban areas by 2050[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR11 "United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs, World Urbanization Prospects: The 2018 Revision (United Nations, 2019), 
                  https://doi.org/10.18356/b9e995fe-en
                  
                ."), it is both urgent and crucial to investigate the trends and patterns of UHI changes to ensure human and environmental well-being in the urban context.

UHI can be influenced by various socio-economic and biophysical factors[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR12 "Yuan, Y. et al. Natural-anthropogenic environment interactively causes the surface urban heat island intensity variations in global climate zones. Environ. Int. 170, 107574 (2022)."), which can vary across countries and climate zones, and modulate the UHI’s seasonal and diurnal variability[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR13 "Chakraborty, T., Hsu, A., Manya, D. & Sheriff, G. A spatially explicit surface urban heat island database for the United States: Characterization, uncertainties, and possible applications. ISPRS J. Photogramm. Remote Sens. 168, 74–88 (2020)."). The intensity and trend of UHI vary across different countries or cities depending on factors such as land use, anthropogenic heat, and climatic background differences[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR14 "Rajagopal, P., Priya, R. S. & Senthil, R. A review of recent developments in the impact of environmental measures on urban heat island. Sustain. Cities Soc. 88, 104279 (2023)."). Cities in tropical and subtropical regions are more susceptible to UHI compared to those in colder climates[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR15 "Arnfield, A. J. Two decades of urban climate research: a review of turbulence, exchanges of energy and water, and the urban heat island. Int. J. Climatol. 23, 1–26 (2003)."), while higher-latitude cities often exhibit distinct seasonal variations[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR16 "Si, M. L. et al. Spatiotemporal pattern and long-term trend of global surface urban heat islands characterized by dynamic urban-extent method and MODIS data. ISPRS J. Photogramm. Remote Sens. 183, 321–335 (2022)."). Over the past four decades, urban heatwave exposure has increased disproportionately in less developed regions compared to developed economies[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR17 "Alizadeh, M. R. et al. Increasing heat-stress inequality in a warming climate. Earths Future 10, e2021EF002488 (2022)."), highlighting the role of economic disparities in shaping UHI dynamics[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR18 "Feng, R. D., Wang, F. Y., Wang, K. Y., Wang, H. J. & Li, L. Urban ecological land and natural-anthropogenic environment interactively drive surface urban heat island: an urban agglomeration-level study in China. Env. Int. 157, 106857 (2021)."),[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR19 "Yu, Z. W., Yao, Y. W., Yang, G. Y., Wang, X. R. & Vejre, H. Strong contribution of rapid urbanization and urban agglomeration development to regional thermal environment dynamics and evolution. For. Ecol. Manag. 446, 214–225 (2019)."),[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR20 "Li, Y., Sun, Y. W., Li, J. L. & Gao, C. Socioeconomic drivers of urban heat island effect: Empirical evidence from major Chinese cities. Sustain. Cities Soc. 63, 102425 (2020)."). In the background of rapid global urbanization in recent decades[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR21 "Grimm, N. B. et al. Global change and the ecology of cities. Science 319, 756–760 (2008)."),[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR22 "Acuto, M. Global science for city policy. Science 359, 165–166 (2018)."), Sun and Chen[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR23 "Sun, L. Q., Chen, J. & Li, Q. L. Huang, D. Dramatic uneven urbanization of large cities throughout the world in recent decades. Nat. Commun. 11, 5366 (2020).") found an uneven distribution of urbanization across different economic levels, cities in low-income and lower-middle-income countries had the highest urban population growth, while built area expansion in upper-middle-income countries was more than three times that of high-income countries. These disparities suggest that UHI intensification may vary significantly across economies, with potentially greater impacts in rapidly urbanizing regions.

Environmental degradation poses severe challenges for low-income countries with limited adaptive capacity, making them more vulnerable to its adverse impacts[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR24 "Field, C. B. & Barros, V. R. Climate Change 2014—Impacts, Adaptation and Vulnerability: Regional Aspects (Cambridge University Press, 2014)."). These countries often face resource constraints and weak infrastructure[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR25 "Zografos, C., Anguelovski, I. & Grigorova, M. When exposure to climate change is not enough: exploring heatwave adaptive capacity of a multi-ethnic, low-income urban community in Australia. Urban Clim. 17, 248–265 (2016)."), which hinder their ability to cope with climate risks[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR26 "Choudhary, B. K., Tripathi, A. K. & Rai, J. Can ‘poor’ cities breathe: responses to climate change in low-income countries. Urban Clim. 27, 403–411 (2019)."),[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR27 "Campbell, S., Remenyi, T. A., White, C. J. & Johnston, F. H. Heatwave and health impact research: a global review. Health Place 53, 210–218 (2018)."), leaving certain vulnerable groups under greater threat from heat stress[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR28 "Chow, W. T. L., Chuang, W. C. & Gober, P. Vulnerability to extreme heat in metropolitan phoenix: spatial, temporal, and demographic dimensions. Prof. Geogr. 64, 286–302 (2012)."),[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR29 "Tang, J. M., Di, L. P., Xiao, J. F., Lu, D. S. & Zhou, Y. Y. Impacts of land use and socioeconomic patterns on urban heat Island. Int. J. Remote Sens. 38, 3445–3465 (2017)."). Identifying the inequalities in UHI exposure crucial[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR30 "Zhu, Y. H. et al. Formulating operational mitigation options and examining intra-urban social inequality using evidence-based urban warming effects. Front. Env. Sci. 9, 795474 (2022)."). However, current research often focuses on urban development levels while neglecting global disparities in urbanization and resource allocation[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR20 "Li, Y., Sun, Y. W., Li, J. L. & Gao, C. Socioeconomic drivers of urban heat island effect: Empirical evidence from major Chinese cities. Sustain. Cities Soc. 63, 102425 (2020)."),[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR31 "Mitchell, B. C. & Chakraborty, J. Landscapes of thermal inequity: disproportionate exposure to urban heat in the three largest US cities. Env. Res. Lett. 10, 115005 (2015)."),[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR32 "Shahfahad et al. Modelling urban heat island (UHI) and thermal field variation and their relationship with land use indices over Delhi and Mumbai metro cities. Environ. Dev. Sustain. 24, 3762–3790 (2022)."). The lack of comprehensive global comparative studies across income levels and climatic zones limits our understanding of UHI impacts in diverse contexts. To address this gap, a broader international perspective is urgently needed to uncover these inequalities and vulnerabilities[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR33 "Schipper, E. L. F. Climate change adaptation and development: exploring the linkages. Tyndall Cent. Clim. Change Res. Working Pap. 107, 13 (2007)."), enabling the development of targeted adaptation measures to mitigate UHI’s adverse effects on at-risk regions.

The Surface Urban Heat Island effect (SUHI) is defined as the variation in land surface temperature between an urban area and its adjacent rural surroundings[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR34 "Hu, J. et al. Spatial patterns and temporal variations of footprint and intensity of surface urban heat island in 141 China cities. Sustain. Cities Soc. 77, 103585 (2022)."). It is derived from remote sensing, providing reliable observations over various spatial and temporal scales[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR16 "Si, M. L. et al. Spatiotemporal pattern and long-term trend of global surface urban heat islands characterized by dynamic urban-extent method and MODIS data. ISPRS J. Photogramm. Remote Sens. 183, 321–335 (2022)."). Studies have established strong correlations between LSTs and near-surface air temperatures[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR35 "Marzban, F., Sodoudi, S. & Preusker, R. The influence of land-cover type on the relationship between NDVI–LST and LST-Tair. Int. J. Remote Sens. 39, 1377–1398 (2018)."), leading SUHI to widespread acceptance and extensive research in the UHI field[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR36 "Li, Y. Z. et al. Surface urban heat islands in 932 urban region agglomerations in China during the morning and before midnight: spatial-temporal changes, drivers, and simulation. Geocarto Int. 37, 13689–13710 (2022)."),[37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR37 "Peng, S. S. et al. Surface Urban Heat Island across 419 global big cities. Environ. Sci. Technol. 46, 696–703 (2012)."),[38](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR38 "Mehmood, M. S. et al. Time series analyses and forecasting of surface urban heat island intensity using ARIMA model in Punjab, Pakistan. Land 12, 142 (2023)."). Our research seeks to address two key questions: (1) What are the predominant UHI trends when differentiated by countries falling in different income country groups and climate zones? (2) Which income country groups are most negatively affected by the intensified UHI effect? To answer these questions, we quantify the changes in surface urban heat island intensity (SUHII) from 2003 to 2018, deliberately excluding the external impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic. Our analysis spans over 9500 urban clusters on a global scale, with detailed insights down to the pixel level. Distinctively, our study employs a grid-scale methodology, diverging from traditional SUHII research that often generalizes across entire cities[39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR39 "Kotharkar, R. & Bagade, A. Evaluating urban heat island in the critical local climate zones of an Indian city. Landsc. Urban Plan. 169, 92–104 (2018)."),[40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR40 "Huang, W. T. K. et al. Economic valuation of temperature-related mortality attributed to urban heat islands in European cities. Nat. Commun. 14, 7438 (2023)."),[41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR41 "Hsu, A., Sheriff, G., Chakraborty, T. & Manya, D. Disproportionate exposure to urban heat island intensity across major US cities. Nat. Commun. 12, 2721 (2021)."),[42](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR42 "Zhou, D. C., Zhao, S. Q., Liu, S. G., Zhang, L. X. & Zhu, C. Surface urban heat island in China’s 32 major cities: spatial patterns and drivers. Remote Sens. Environ. 152, 51–61 (2014)."). This study examines variations in cities by country and income group, capturing even extreme changes. By focusing on urban heterogeneity, it moves beyond treating cities as uniform entities, offering a clearer understanding of SUHII's spatial dynamics. The research underscores the urgent need to address climate vulnerabilities amid global economic disparities, ensuring urban thermal complexities are fully considered in discussions on urban climate resilience. It also provides a foundation for developing targeted strategies to mitigate and adapt to UHI impacts.

Results
-------

### Evolving dynamics of global SUHII

Our findings reveal that SUHII increases more rapidly during the daytime in summer and nighttime in winter. As shown in Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#Tab1), the order of mean δSUHII values at a different time is: summer daytime (SD, 0.033 ± 0.094 °C/yr) > yearly daytime (YD, 0.021 ± 0.072 °C/yr) > winter nighttime (WN, 0.018 ± 0.058 °C/yr) > summer nighttime (SN, 0.014 ± 0.042 °C/yr) => yearly nighttime (YN, 0.012 ± 0.038 °C/yr) > winter daytime (WD, 0.006 ± 0.085 °C/yr). The δSUHII’s standard deviation during the daytime exceeds that of nighttime. This can likely be attributed to the joint effects of multiple factors that influence SUHII formation during the daytime, resulting in more pronounced variability[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#ref-CR12 "Yuan, Y. et al. Natural-anthropogenic environment interactively causes the surface urban heat island intensity variations in global climate zones. Environ. Int. 170, 107574 (2022).").

**Table 1 The summary of global SUHII changing rates (δSUHII, °C/yr) seasonally and diurnally**

[Full size table](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9/tables/1)

The maximal variations of SUHII are observed in different countries at different times and income levels (Table [S1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#MOESM1)). On an annual scale, Iran (upper-middle income) has the maximum decrease rate of −0.451 °C/yr, and China (upper-middle income) has the maximum increase rate of 0.484 °C/yr in the daytime. However, China has the maximum decrease of −0.393 °C/yr, while France (high income) has the maximum increase of 0.427 °C/yr in the nighttime. The rates of maximum increase and decrease in SUHII are predominantly observed in the high and upper-middle-income country groups, but Nepal (low-income) exhibits the most significant increase, with a maximum rise of 0.432 °C/yr during the summer night. Notably, all medium (50%) values of δSUHII are greater than 0 (Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#Tab1)**)**, indicating that the majority of urban grids experienced an increasing SUHII trend from 2003 to 2018.

The spatial patterns of extreme SUHII trends vary substantially across the globe. Across all periods, more than 70% of urban grids experience SUHII change rates within the range of −0.1 to 0.1 °C/yr, with even over 92% falling within this range at night (Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#Tab1) and Fig. [S1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#MOESM1)). Consequently, we select δSUHII values with an absolute value greater than 0.1 °C/yr within a 95% confidence interval (_p_< 0.05) as the extreme changing rates (Fig. [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#Fig1) and Fig. [S2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9#MOESM1)). During the daytime, significant increases in SUHII are observed in the Midwest and Southern regions of the US, Japan, and southeastern China. Similarly, rising trends are particularly prominent in large areas of eastern and southern Africa, as well as Europe. Conversely, most parts of the Middle East show decreasing trends, and a similar phenomenon is observed in Northeastern China during winter. In the nighttime, significant upward trends are observed in Eastern China, particularly during winter, while some areas in Europe exhibit a decreasing trend during this season.

**Fig. 1: Significant Global SUHII change rates over 2003–2018.**

[![Image 5: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs42949-025-00198-9/MediaObjects/42949_2025_198_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s42949-025-00198-9/figures/1)

The changing rates δSUHII over 0.1 °C per year within a 95% confidence interval (_p_< 0.05) are selected as the extreme changing rates at: **a** yearly daytime (YD) and **b** yearly nighttime (YN). The violin plot in the lower left corner displays the distribution of rates for total decreasing and increasing trends. The red box highlights the large area where SUHII shows an upward trend, while the green box highlights the large area where SUHII shows a downward trend.

DESCRIPTION: The illustration compares the heat absorption and retention differences between rural areas with vegetation and water evaporation, which are less affected, and urban environments with dense buildings that are 3° to 10°C hotter due to the urban heat island effect caused by increased heat absorption and reduced plant transpiration.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Low-income communities experience heightened temperatures due to urban heat islands, increasing health risks and energy burdens, often exacerbating existing social inequalities.
Many vulnerable populations lack access to air conditioning and cooling centers, leaving them more susceptible to heat-related illnesses during extreme heat events.
Social inequities, such as limited financial resources and inadequate housing, amplify the impacts of urban heat islands, disproportionately affecting marginalized groups and communities of color.
